paper_title: Tobacco Exposure and Risk of Developing Fuchs Endothelial Corneal Dystrophy in the Women's Health Initiative Studies.
raw_limitation_text:
[DISCUSSION]
We used a large data set derived from the WHI OS and CTs to examine associations between personal tobacco and SHS exposure in relation to FECD risk, with detailed parameterization of tobacco exposure. This analysis was conducted in postmenopausal women, the group most impacted by FECD. Overall, we did not find that tobacco exposure, either via personal cigarette or SHS exposure, was associated with an increased risk of developing FECD in postmenopausal women. Most of our findings were not statistically significant, although our results were suggestive of an increased risk for some exposure groups. We observed a 12% (HR: 1.12, 95% CIs: 0.90–1.38) increase in risk of developing FECD among current smokers compared with never smokers. In a sensitivity analysis, we observed that current smokers who smoked at the highest intensity, ≥15 cigarettes/day, had a 26% (HR: 1.26, 95% CI: 0.94–1.68) increase in FECD risk compared with never smokers. Similarly, never smokers exposed to SHS at home during childhood only or adulthood only had approximately a 22%–25% increased risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers with no SHS exposure.

Unexpectedly, we also found an inverse relationship between both average cigarettes per day and smoking pack-years in relation to FECD risk. Women who smoke/smoked at the highest intensity (≥25 cigarettes/day) had an 11% nonsignificant lower risk of FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.75–1.06). Women with the highest smoking pack-years, a measure of intensity and duration, also had a lower risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.79–1.00). It is possible that this sample of heavy smokers who lived to be enrolled into WHI, may be different from heavy smokers in the general population who may have been too ill to join WHI; although, this is speculation. While it is outside of the scope of this study, future researchers could consider utilizing trial emulation designs to explore the impacts of smoking on FECD.

We also found an insignificant decreased risk of FECD (HR: 0.92, 95% CIs: 0.84–1.01) among former compared with never smokers. Former smokers who smoked ≥15 cigarettes per day had a significantly lower risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.86, 95% CIs: 0.76–0.97). These results contradicted our hypothesis, and we are unaware of any biological explanation for this finding. We speculated that duration since quitting may have clouded the effect estimates, as it is possible that former smokers who quit decades ago would have effect estimates more similar to never smokers, whereas smokers who recently quit may have effect estimates closer to current smokers. We utilized the approximate age at which women quit smoking to explore duration since quitting, and our results did not change. Upon stratifying former smokers by reason for quitting (i.e., due to a health concern, yes/no) our results remained consistent. Our former smoker analyses may also have been impacted by survival biases or few FECD cases, potentially making our sample of former smokers somehow different than the general population. It is possible that former smokers made lifestyle changes after they quit smoking (prior to our study baseline) that substantially improved their health and helped counteract past damage from smoking. We also explored whether the main associations differed by comorbidity status. We assumed that those with comorbidities—specifically diabetes—were more likely to be referred to and closely monitored by an eye doctor. Stratifying by comorbidity status may serve as an imperfect proxy for eye care utilization that would result in higher rates of outcome detection. However, our study found no effect modification by comorbidity status.

Previous research examining personal cigarette smoking has observed increased odds of prevalent FECD or FECD severity. In a study of White individuals within the FECD Genetics Multi-Center Study cohort, Zhang et al examined smoking and FECD severity, finding that smoking (i.e., ever vs. never smoker) was associated with increased FECD severity (odds ratio: 1.30, 95% CIs: 1.00–1.68). However, their study did not assess pack-years smoked and was unable to tease apart current versus former smokers, which our study was able to address. A small study, conducted in Tangier Island (N = 156), observed more people with a history of regularly smoking tobacco among those with FECD compared with those without, but these results also were not statistically significant. An Icelandic study of adults aged ≥55 years examined the association between having smoked ≥20 pack-years and prevalence of FECD and found that smoking was associated with an increased prevalence of FECD (odds ratio: 2.20, 95% CIs: 1.18–4.10). However, their study did not adjust for confounding variables. In our study, we were able to adjust for a number of likely confounders. Other researchers have observed inverse associations more similar to ours. A cross-sectional study, conducted in Japan, found a nonsignificant protective association between smoking and FECD (odds ratio: 0.67, 95% CIs: 0.40–1.12).

Different findings across these studies and ours may be explained by factors such as genetic heterogeneity, prevalence of smoking in the population, or prevalence of FECD. For example, the prevalence of FECD in the Icelandic study was 11% among women, and the prevalence was roughly 4.1% in total in the Japanese study, whereas our study had an incidence of 6.1% among women at risk for the disease at the start of our study. We also were unable to examine FECD severity and were confined to only identifying incident cases of any endothelial corneal dystrophy. Furthermore, many of our participants were never smokers (55.0%) and were exposed to SHS at some point in their lifetime (93.6%). It is possible that a lack of variation in tobacco exposure, and a lack of data on FECD severity, potentially inhibited our ability to obtain statistically significant associations. It should be noted that our results and many of the aforementioned previous findings have failed to demonstrate that there was a significantly elevated risk for FECD due to smoking.

Next, we attempted to explore the association between SHS exposure and FECD risk by personal smoking status (current or former) and pack-years (low or high pack-years). We hypothesized that current smokers or smokers with more pack-years exposed to SHS would have an increased risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers with SHS exposure. However, we could not conduct meaningful analyses of stratified data due to sparse data issues. It remains unclear if the association with SHS and FECD risk is impacted by personal smoking exposure. To our knowledge, there have been no prior publications exploring SHS exposure and prospective risk of FECD, limiting our ability to compare our results to previous literature.

This study has multiple strengths. Our study sample consisted of older women, the demographic group most impacted by FECD. We were able to characterize personal cigarette exposure in multiple ways, including current smoking status, smoking pack-years, and the average number of cigarettes per day during smoking years. This robust utilization of the exposure allowed us to examine multiple aspects of personal cigarette use. To our knowledge, previous studies of FECD were not able to conduct as detailed exposure parameterization as this study. Next, the prospective nature of this study has several benefits. Prospective study designs contribute evidence that supports a causal association. Even if the reporting of smoking or SHS exposure was biased, it would likely be nondifferential in nature due to the prospective outcome ascertainment, attenuating our risk estimates. Combining the OS and CTs allowed us to increase our sample size and a standard protocol for combing the data was followed, which included adjusting for study design and randomization status. We also conducted time-varying analyses, allowing for smoking status to change over time. Our results remained similar in magnitude and direction to the main results, alleviating concerns of exposure misclassification due to real changes in behavior. Additionally, our extensive covariate data allowed for the thoughtful adjustment of potential confounding factors, which was a limitation of prior literature.

Our study is not without limitations. The WHI did not have data on FECD severity or genetic predisposition, limiting further exploration of these 2 aspects of the disease. Our sample was primarily White, well-educated, US women, which may limit the generalizability to our target population of all US women. We had a small sample of current smokers, which limited our power for detecting associations in smokers compared with never smokers. The biological mechanisms hypothesized to link tobacco exposure and FECD risk would not differ by study population, suggesting that our results may be applicable to other groups. The outcome ascertainment was conducted utilizing Medicare data, which were originally collected for payment and administration purposes and have known limitations. Similarly, FECD can go undiagnosed for a considerable amount of time, which could be a source of outcome misclassification in this study. When FECD cases diagnosed within 5 years of baseline were excluded, we found similar findings. Next, we were unable to rule out the possibility that survival or sick-quitter biases occurred. Because smoking is a harmful health behavior, those sickest from smoking may die of other causes before having the chance to enroll in the WHI or develop FECD, a disease that is more prevalent in older adults. In the US, it is estimated that smokers die on average 10 years before never smokers, supporting the possibility of survival bias impacting our findings. We lacked variation in SHS exposure; >90% of women were exposed to SHS during >1 period of their life. The lack of variation in SHS exposure likely limited our ability to find meaningful results for this secondary outcome. Similarly, other airborne environmental exposures can impact eye health, but we did not have access to data to investigate this potential confoudner. Lastly, by including data from the OS and CTs, we may have introduced unnecessary variation into our analyses, but study design and randomization status were controlled for. Tobacco exposure is associated with many other health conditions and should be avoided for other reasons, regardless of our findings.

Our study findings contribute to the limited epidemiologic literature on modifiable risk factors and prospective risk of FECD. In this sample of postmenopausal women, personal smoking and SHS exposure were not significantly associated with an increased risk of FECD. However, our findings indicated there may be an association between tobacco exposure and FECD but limitations in the variation of tobacco exposure in WHI, survival bias, and sick quitter effects may have contributed to wide CIs. Despite these challenges, our study is one of the most robust analyses to date regarding tobacco exposure and risk of FECD among older women. Modifiable exposures warrant further exploration in relation to FECD risk and severity.

[CONCLUSION]
In this sample of postmenopausal women, personal smoking and SHS were not significantly associated with increased risk of FECD, but suggestions of an increased risk were observed in current smokers. Our findings may have been impacted by lack of variation in exposures, survival, and sick-quitter biases.

Financial Disclosure(s)

Proprietary or commercial disclosure may be found in the Footnotes and Disclosures at the end of this article.

Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD) is characterized by the progressive dysfunction and death of corneal endothelial cells. Corneal endothelial cells aid in the exchange of water and nutrients from the adjacent corneal stromal tissue and aqueous humor, allowing the cornea to maintain hydration necessary for transparency. Estimations of FECD prevalence and incidence in the United States (US) population are limited, but a recent meta-analysis estimated the global prevalence of FECD to be approximately 7.3%. Individuals impacted by FECD may experience a variety of progressively worsening symptoms including nighttime glare, decreased visual acuity, decreased contrast sensitivity, and eye pain.

Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy likely develops from a combination of genetic and environmental factors. While multiple genetic loci have been associated with FECD, the disease shows variable expression within families, and genetic etiologies have not been identified for many. This suggests that additional potentially modifiable factors may contribute to the development of FECD or exacerbation of the condition. The major known nongenetic risk factors for FECD include female sex and older age. Currently, the literature on modifiable risk factors of FECD, such as smoking, ultraviolet light exposure, diabetes, postmenopausal hormone replacement use, and body mass index (BMI) is sparse.

Smoking is a well-established risk factor for many chronic health conditions, including other chronic eye diseases such as cataracts and age-related macular degeneration, but insufficient evidence exists linking tobacco exposure and FECD risk. Smokers’ eyes are consistently and directly exposed to cigarette smoke, and tobacco exposure also has damaging systemic influences. There are multiple defined pathways such as increased inflammation and DNA damage that can occur due to tobacco exposure. A few studies have examined personal smoking habits and the risk or prevalence of FECD, FECD severity, or presence of central cornea guttae (i.e., a hallmark of FECD) and found that smoking was likely associated with FECD-related outcomes. In contrast, a cross-sectional study in Japan found a nonsignificant, protective association between smoking and FECD. Few studies have examined the role of secondhand smoking (SHS) (i.e., passive or environmental smoke exposure) and ocular conditions. Secondhand smoking is directly associated with a variety of health problems including diabetes, eye irritation, and dry eye symptoms. Older individuals (>65 years of age) may be particularly vulnerable to the effects of SHS exposure because the prevalence of other health conditions (i.e., breathing problems and heart diseases) also generally increases with age. To our knowledge, no studies have examined SHS and FECD explicitly. A better understanding of the role of smoking, inclusive of SHS, in FECD is especially pertinent, as approximately 11.5% of the US adults currently smoke.

The objective of this research was twofold. First, we examined the association between personal smoking and risk of FECD in the Women’s Health Initiative (WHI) Observational Study (OS) and Clinical Trials (CTs). We hypothesized that current and former smokers would have a higher risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers. Second, we examined whether SHS exposure was associated with FECD risk in the WHI OS. We hypothesized never smokers exposed to SHS would have a higher risk of developing FECD compared with those not exposed to SHS, and that current and former smokers exposed to SHS would have a higher risk of developing FECD compared with those not exposed to SHS.

Methods

Study Population

We used data from the WHI—including participants from the OS and CTs. Participants from both the OS and CTs were included to increase overall sample size and study power and provide us with the opportunity to explore SHS, which was only collected in the OS. The WHI OS aimed to study risk factors and diseases that contribute to high morbidity and mortality in postmenopausal women. Between 1993 and 1998, the WHI OS enrolled 93 676 postmenopausal women between the ages of 50 and 79 from 40 health centers across the United States The WHI CTs were conducted to explore the benefits and risks of hormone therapy (HT), a low-fat diet, and calcium and vitamin D supplementation in 68 132 postmenopausal women aged 50-79 years. In the OS and CTs, all required institutional review board/ethics committee approvals were obtained and all included participants provided written informed consent. All research adhered to the tenets of the Declaration of Helsinki.

We included participants who were ≥65 years old at WHI baseline, enrolled in Medicare part B, and remained continuously enrolled in WHI and Medicare for ≥1 year after baseline. We excluded women missing baseline data on smoking exposures or pertinent confounders and women with ≥1 diagnosis of corneal endothelial dystrophy, corneal transplants, or corneal failure/rejection/complications within the Medicare claims data during the first year after WHI enrollment. Incident cases of FECD were identified after 1 year of WHI enrollment through 2019. Women from both the OS and CTs were included for analyses looking at personal cigarette smoking exposure (n = 37 824) (Supplemental Figure 1, available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org). For analyses examining SHS, only women in the OS were included (n = 22 301) as SHS exposure was not assessed in the CTs.

Outcome

An incident FECD diagnosis, identified using fee-for-service claims from the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services data within >1 year after WHI enrollment through 2019, was the primary outcome. International Classification of Diseases (ICD) 9 and ICD10 codes (371.57, H18.51) for FECD were utilized to identify cases within the Medicare data (Medicare part B, not including managed care). Corneal transplant surgery or transplant failure/rejection/complications were identified with ICD9 codes (V42.5 and 996.51) or ICD10 codes (Z94.7, T86.840, T86.841, T86.842, T86.848). Participants with incident corneal transplant or corneal failure/rejection/complications without an incident FECD diagnosis were excluded. Our sample consisted of n = 2305 incident FECD cases (n = 1379 in the OS).

Exposure

Personal Cigarette Smoking

At baseline, WHI OS and CTs participants were asked 8 questions regarding smoking, and during WHI follow-up, 2 smoking questions were assessed up to 7 times for the WHI OS participants and up to 5 times for the WHI CTs participants (Supplemental Table 1, available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org). These questions were utilized to create variables including smoking status, smoking pack-years, and the average number of cigarettes per day during smoking years. Former smokers were asked to report the 5-year age category when they quit smoking (i.e., How old were you when you quit smoking regularly?).

SHS

Secondhand smoke exposure was only assessed in WHI OS participants at baseline and follow-up years 3 and 6. At baseline, participants were asked if they had been exposed to SHS in the workplace (as an adult) or at home (as a child or adult) (Supplemental Table 1). During follow-up years 3 and 6, participants were asked questions to update SHS exposure. A composite SHS score was created by combining participants’ SHS exposure in childhood at home, adulthood at home, and adulthood in the workplace as previously done in WHI. For former and current smokers, we utilized a binary SHS variable (none vs. any at home or workplace) due to sparse data issues to examine if SHS compounded FECD risk in those who already smoke(d).

Covariates

All covariate data were from WHI baseline data collection. Height and weight were measured by clinic staff, and these data were used to calculate BMI using kg/m2. Participant age, education, self-identified race, ethnicity, marital status, and HT use were assessed via self-report. Participants also self-reported comorbidities including hypertension (i.e., “Did a doctor ever say that you had hypertension or high blood pressure?”), cardiovascular diseases (i.e., “Has a doctor ever told you that you had heart problems, problems with your blood circulation, or blood clots?”) and diabetes status (i.e., “Did a doctor ever say that you had sugar diabetes or high blood sugar when you were not pregnant?”).

Statistical Analysis

To describe participant baseline characteristics, we calculated frequencies (%) for categorical and means and standard deviations for continuous variables for the total sample and by personal smoking status and SHS smoking status exposure categories. Some data by characteristic or stratification were not shown to comply with the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services cell size policy. Person-time was calculated from WHI baseline until the first occurrence of (1) an FECD diagnosis, (2) a participant unenrolled from Medicare part B for the first time, (3) death from any cause, or (4) the end of the study/last follow-up time (through 2019). Given our sample sizes and number of events, with an alpha of 0.05, and a 2-sided test, we had approximately 80% power to observe a hazard ratio (HR) of ≥1.31 and 1.13 among current and former smokers, respectively, compared with never smokers, and a HR of 1.37 in women ever exposed to SHS compared with never exposed.

Personal Cigarette Smoking Analyses

Cox proportional hazards (PHs) regression models were used to estimate HR and corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for incident FECD among current and past smokers compared with never smokers at baseline. P for trend calculations were conducted using the continuous form of the respective variables (i.e., pack-years smoked and average cigarettes per day). Final models were adjusted for WHI study design (i.e., OS vs. CTs) and individual CT arm (randomization status) to account for potential differences between the cohorts. Additional covariates for adjustment were identified using the disjunctive cause criterion concept and prior knowledge of the field. The disjunctive cause criterion aims to identify covariates that are a cause of the exposure, the outcome, or both the exposure and outcome. Covariates included in the adjusted models were age, education, race, BMI, marital status, HT use, and self-reported diabetes status at baseline. All PH models were tested for the PH assumption using Schoenfeld residuals. Age, education, study design (OS or CTs), individual CT arms, and HT use variables violated the PH assumption and were included as hazard stratification variables.

Potential covariates that may lead to variations in the observed associations were identified a priori based on knowledge of the field. We stratified former smoker and FECD analyses by smoking-quitting status (i.e., quit due to a health problem made worse or caused by smoking: no/yes) to address “sick quitter” concerns, because sick quitters may differ in survival and study enrollment. We also created a derived variable indicating time from quitting until WHI baseline, based on self-reported age at quitting and age at baseline. Given that age at quitting was collected categorically, we took the midpoint of the selected age group as the age at quitting (i.e., “<15” = 15, “50-54” = 52, etc.).

As an exploratory analysis, we conducted time-varying Cox PH regression models of the association between smoking status and FECD development to account for potential changes in smoking status over time. Because of the differences in OS and CT data collection, participants completed differing numbers of questionnaires (i.e., exposure measures) over time. In this exploratory analysis, we focused on the OS and the repeat measures within the OS of smoking status, HT use, and BMI. Thus, our time-dependent models assumed these variables (smoking, HT use, and BMI) remained constant from the last completed questionnaire until a new questionnaire was completed (i.e., carryforward method), providing up-to-date exposure data.

In a sensitivity analysis, we stratified the final associations by comorbidity status, using the derived variable representing systemic diseases that impact eye health. We hypothesized that FECD diagnosis may vary due to frequency of eye doctor visits which varies by systemic diseases known to impact eye health. A derived comorbidity variable was used to represent if participants had ever been diagnosed with diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease (yes/no). We also conducted a sensitivity analysis excluding women diagnosed with FECD within the first 5 years and repeated the main analyses, to ensure prevalent cases at baseline were excluded.

SHS Analyses

In the OS cohort, among never smokers, SHS exposure was examined in 2 ways: (1) SHS exposure by location (never, childhood home, adulthood home, or workplace) and (2) SHS as a combination variable (i.e., childhood + adulthood home + workplace). For former and current smokers, a binary SHS variable was utilized (none vs. any at home or workplace) due to sparse data concerns. Cox PH regression models were conducted to estimate HRs (crude and adjusted) and corresponding 95% CIs. Those with no SHS exposure served as the reference group for all analyses. We selected confounders using the aforementioned method. We also explored the association between SHS and the risk of developing FECD by personal pack-years smoked (i.e., low and high based on the median) as we expected the associations to differ based on duration of personal smoking.

Comorbidity status included hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes. This variable was used as a stratifying variable and has missing data (n = 2664 missing).

Study participants’ characteristics for the total analytic sample (N = 37 824) and by baseline smoking status are provided in Table 1. Based on baseline measures, the total sample was mostly non-Hispanic (96.9%) White women (89.7%), with 85.9% having an educational attainment greater than high school, 57.7% presently married or living in a marriage-like relationship, primarily nondiabetic (93.3%), and with 50.6% never having used postmenopausal HT. Most of the women were never smokers (55%), followed by former (40%) and current (5%) smokers. Current smokers were slightly younger (69.2 vs. 70.3 yrs), more likely to be Black Americans (10.2 vs. 5.6%), more likely to have an educational attainment less than high school (7.0 vs. 5.7%), less likely to be married (41.7 vs. 59.3%), more likely to have a lower BMI (26.1 vs. 27.5 kg/m2), and more likely to have never used HT (57.0 vs. 51.7%) compared with never smokers. The prevalence of self-reported diabetes was similar across groups. In our sample, roughly 6.1% women developed FECD during the study period. Overall, roughly 61% of the sample originated from the WHI OS. The cumulative probability of developing incident FECD by smoking status is illustrated in Supplemental Figure 2, available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org.

CI = confidence interval; CTs = clinical trials; FECD = Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy; HR = hazard ratio; OS = observational study; WHI = Women’s Health Initiative.

Models were adjusted for, or included, the following covariates as hazard stratification variables: age, education, race, body mass index, marital status, postmenopausal hormone use, self-reported diabetes status, study design (OS vs. CTs), and randomization to individual CTs arms.

Categories were created based on the mean value for duration since quitting.

P for trend calculated using the continuous form of the variable.

The time-varying analysis was conducted in the OS only.

While not statistically significant, in the final model, current smokers on average had a 12% increase in risk of FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 1.12, 95% CIs: 0.90–1.38) (Table 2). The risk of FECD in former smokers was lower compared with never smokers, but did not reach statistical significance in the adjusted model. However, the risk did not differ in those who did and did not quit smoking due to health concerns or by duration since quitting. In our stratified analyses, former smokers who smoked at the highest intensity (≥25 cigarettes/day) had an 11% reduction in FECD risk compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.75–1.06). Similarly, the women with the highest smoking pack-years, a measure of intensity and duration, had a reduced risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.79–1.00). In time-varying analyses allowing for smoking status to change over time, the results remained similar in magnitude and direction to the main results.

CI = confidence interval; CTs = clinical trials; FECD = Fuchs endothelial corneal dystrophy; HR = hazard ratio; OS = observational study; WHI = Women’s Health Initiative.

Models were adjusted for, or included, the following covariates as hazard stratification variables: age, education, race, body mass index, marital status, menopausal hormone therapy use status, self-reported diabetes status, study design (OS vs. CTs), and randomization to individual CTs arms.

Adjusted for all covariates as noted in footnote “∗” except for diabetes status.

In sensitivity analyses, we stratified the adjusted models for average cigarettes per day, smoking pack-years, and comorbidity status by smoking status (Table 3). Current smokers who smoked ≥15 cigarettes/day had a 26% increase in risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR = 1.26, 95% CIs: 0.94–1.68). However, among former smokers who smoked ≥15 cigarettes/day, there was a 14% reduced risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR = 0.86, 95% CIs: 0.76–0.97). Upon examining smoking pack-years by smoking status, in baseline current smokers, we observed elevated risks of developing FECD compared with never smokers. In former smokers compared with never smokers, those with a greater smoking pack-years (>10 years) were associated with a 10% decreased risk of FECD (HR = 0.90, 95% CIs: 0.81–1.00).

The risk of FECD in current and former smokers compared with never smokers was examined in those with and without a comorbidity. Former smoker effect estimates did not differ by comorbidity status, producing the same effect estimate across both strata compared with never smokers. Current smokers with comorbidities had an increased risk in FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 1.21, 95% CIs: 0.89–1.65). The overlapping CIs within the comparable groups suggest that there is no effect modification by comorbidity status. Furthermore, excluding women diagnosed with FECD within the first 5 years did not meaningfully change our findings, but statistical significance was reached for 2 associations among former smokers (Supplemental Table 2, available at www.ophthalmologyscience.org).

Any SHS variable includes any child or adulthood in home exposure or workplace exposure.

We examined the characteristics of WHI OS participants by SHS exposure (no SHS vs. Any SHS) (Table 4). Most women were exposed to SHS at some time in their lifetime (93.6%). Compared with women who were not exposed to SHS, women with any SHS were more likely to be non-Hispanic or Latina (97.4 vs. 94.4%), White (90.8 vs. 88.5%), and either current or former HT users. Women with any SHS were less likely to have graduated from college (11.3 vs. 16.0%) and never married (4.6 vs. 6.5%) than women without SHS exposure. Generally, the mean age of participants and BMI was similar across SHS groups (70.7 vs. 70.2 years and 26.5 vs. 26.9 kg/m2, respectively). There were no significant differences in the prevalence of diabetes by SHS status in this sample.

Models were adjusted for, or included, the following covariates as hazard stratification variables: age, education, race, body mass index, marital status, postmenopausal hormone use, and self-reported diabetes status.

Any SHS variable includes any childhood or adulthood in home exposure and workplace exposure.

No statistically significant associations between SHS and FECD risk were seen in this sample of postmenopausal women (Table 5). While not significant, never smokers who were exposed consistently at home during childhood only or adulthood only had approximately a 22%-25% increase in risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers with no SHS exposure. The associations for SHS exposure in adulthood at work only, childhood + adult home, childhood + adult work, and all 3 SHS locations combined were inverse and nonsignificant in nature, opposite of our proposed hypothesis. Overall, we had sparse data issues due to the lack of variation in SHS exposure and limited cases of FECD in this sample. When we attempted to examine the impact of SHS on risk of FECD among current smokers the models did not converge due to zero cases of FECD among current smokers with no SHS exposure. Small sample size concerns also impacted our results among former smokers and those with low and high pack-years. However, we did see that among individuals who had lower pack-years smoked, the risk of FECD was increased (HR = 1.07, 95% CIs: 0.53–2.19) and that the association’s magnitude increased among those with higher pack-years smoked (HR = 1.17, 95% CIs: 0.37–3.66).

Discussion

We used a large data set derived from the WHI OS and CTs to examine associations between personal tobacco and SHS exposure in relation to FECD risk, with detailed parameterization of tobacco exposure. This analysis was conducted in postmenopausal women, the group most impacted by FECD. Overall, we did not find that tobacco exposure, either via personal cigarette or SHS exposure, was associated with an increased risk of developing FECD in postmenopausal women. Most of our findings were not statistically significant, although our results were suggestive of an increased risk for some exposure groups. We observed a 12% (HR: 1.12, 95% CIs: 0.90–1.38) increase in risk of developing FECD among current smokers compared with never smokers. In a sensitivity analysis, we observed that current smokers who smoked at the highest intensity, ≥15 cigarettes/day, had a 26% (HR: 1.26, 95% CI: 0.94–1.68) increase in FECD risk compared with never smokers. Similarly, never smokers exposed to SHS at home during childhood only or adulthood only had approximately a 22%–25% increased risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers with no SHS exposure.

Unexpectedly, we also found an inverse relationship between both average cigarettes per day and smoking pack-years in relation to FECD risk. Women who smoke/smoked at the highest intensity (≥25 cigarettes/day) had an 11% nonsignificant lower risk of FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.75–1.06). Women with the highest smoking pack-years, a measure of intensity and duration, also had a lower risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.89, 95% CIs: 0.79–1.00). It is possible that this sample of heavy smokers who lived to be enrolled into WHI, may be different from heavy smokers in the general population who may have been too ill to join WHI; although, this is speculation. While it is outside of the scope of this study, future researchers could consider utilizing trial emulation designs to explore the impacts of smoking on FECD.

We also found an insignificant decreased risk of FECD (HR: 0.92, 95% CIs: 0.84–1.01) among former compared with never smokers. Former smokers who smoked ≥15 cigarettes per day had a significantly lower risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers (HR: 0.86, 95% CIs: 0.76–0.97). These results contradicted our hypothesis, and we are unaware of any biological explanation for this finding. We speculated that duration since quitting may have clouded the effect estimates, as it is possible that former smokers who quit decades ago would have effect estimates more similar to never smokers, whereas smokers who recently quit may have effect estimates closer to current smokers. We utilized the approximate age at which women quit smoking to explore duration since quitting, and our results did not change. Upon stratifying former smokers by reason for quitting (i.e., due to a health concern, yes/no) our results remained consistent. Our former smoker analyses may also have been impacted by survival biases or few FECD cases, potentially making our sample of former smokers somehow different than the general population. It is possible that former smokers made lifestyle changes after they quit smoking (prior to our study baseline) that substantially improved their health and helped counteract past damage from smoking. We also explored whether the main associations differed by comorbidity status. We assumed that those with comorbidities—specifically diabetes—were more likely to be referred to and closely monitored by an eye doctor. Stratifying by comorbidity status may serve as an imperfect proxy for eye care utilization that would result in higher rates of outcome detection. However, our study found no effect modification by comorbidity status.

Previous research examining personal cigarette smoking has observed increased odds of prevalent FECD or FECD severity. In a study of White individuals within the FECD Genetics Multi-Center Study cohort, Zhang et al examined smoking and FECD severity, finding that smoking (i.e., ever vs. never smoker) was associated with increased FECD severity (odds ratio: 1.30, 95% CIs: 1.00–1.68). However, their study did not assess pack-years smoked and was unable to tease apart current versus former smokers, which our study was able to address. A small study, conducted in Tangier Island (N = 156), observed more people with a history of regularly smoking tobacco among those with FECD compared with those without, but these results also were not statistically significant. An Icelandic study of adults aged ≥55 years examined the association between having smoked ≥20 pack-years and prevalence of FECD and found that smoking was associated with an increased prevalence of FECD (odds ratio: 2.20, 95% CIs: 1.18–4.10). However, their study did not adjust for confounding variables. In our study, we were able to adjust for a number of likely confounders. Other researchers have observed inverse associations more similar to ours. A cross-sectional study, conducted in Japan, found a nonsignificant protective association between smoking and FECD (odds ratio: 0.67, 95% CIs: 0.40–1.12).

Different findings across these studies and ours may be explained by factors such as genetic heterogeneity, prevalence of smoking in the population, or prevalence of FECD. For example, the prevalence of FECD in the Icelandic study was 11% among women, and the prevalence was roughly 4.1% in total in the Japanese study, whereas our study had an incidence of 6.1% among women at risk for the disease at the start of our study. We also were unable to examine FECD severity and were confined to only identifying incident cases of any endothelial corneal dystrophy. Furthermore, many of our participants were never smokers (55.0%) and were exposed to SHS at some point in their lifetime (93.6%). It is possible that a lack of variation in tobacco exposure, and a lack of data on FECD severity, potentially inhibited our ability to obtain statistically significant associations. It should be noted that our results and many of the aforementioned previous findings have failed to demonstrate that there was a significantly elevated risk for FECD due to smoking.

Next, we attempted to explore the association between SHS exposure and FECD risk by personal smoking status (current or former) and pack-years (low or high pack-years). We hypothesized that current smokers or smokers with more pack-years exposed to SHS would have an increased risk of developing FECD compared with never smokers with SHS exposure. However, we could not conduct meaningful analyses of stratified data due to sparse data issues. It remains unclear if the association with SHS and FECD risk is impacted by personal smoking exposure. To our knowledge, there have been no prior publications exploring SHS exposure and prospective risk of FECD, limiting our ability to compare our results to previous literature.

This study has multiple strengths. Our study sample consisted of older women, the demographic group most impacted by FECD. We were able to characterize personal cigarette exposure in multiple ways, including current smoking status, smoking pack-years, and the average number of cigarettes per day during smoking years. This robust utilization of the exposure allowed us to examine multiple aspects of personal cigarette use. To our knowledge, previous studies of FECD were not able to conduct as detailed exposure parameterization as this study. Next, the prospective nature of this study has several benefits. Prospective study designs contribute evidence that supports a causal association. Even if the reporting of smoking or SHS exposure was biased, it would likely be nondifferential in nature due to the prospective outcome ascertainment, attenuating our risk estimates. Combining the OS and CTs allowed us to increase our sample size and a standard protocol for combing the data was followed, which included adjusting for study design and randomization status. We also conducted time-varying analyses, allowing for smoking status to change over time. Our results remained similar in magnitude and direction to the main results, alleviating concerns of exposure misclassification due to real changes in behavior. Additionally, our extensive covariate data allowed for the thoughtful adjustment of potential confounding factors, which was a limitation of prior literature.

Our study is not without limitations. The WHI did not have data on FECD severity or genetic predisposition, limiting further exploration of these 2 aspects of the disease. Our sample was primarily White, well-educated, US women, which may limit the generalizability to our target population of all US women. We had a small sample of current smokers, which limited our power for detecting associations in smokers compared with never smokers. The biological mechanisms hypothesized to link tobacco exposure and FECD risk would not differ by study population, suggesting that our results may be applicable to other groups. The outcome ascertainment was conducted utilizing Medicare data, which were originally collected for payment and administration purposes and have known limitations. Similarly, FECD can go undiagnosed for a considerable amount of time, which could be a source of outcome misclassification in this study. When FECD cases diagnosed within 5 years of baseline were excluded, we found similar findings. Next, we were unable to rule out the possibility that survival or sick-quitter biases occurred. Because smoking is a harmful health behavior, those sickest from smoking may die of other causes before having the chance to enroll in the WHI or develop FECD, a disease that is more prevalent in older adults. In the US, it is estimated that smokers die on average 10 years before never smokers, supporting the possibility of survival bias impacting our findings. We lacked variation in SHS exposure; >90% of women were exposed to SHS during >1 period of their life. The lack of variation in SHS exposure likely limited our ability to find meaningful results for this secondary outcome. Similarly, other airborne environmental exposures can impact eye health, but we did not have access to data to investigate this potential confoudner. Lastly, by including data from the OS and CTs, we may have introduced unnecessary variation into our analyses, but study design and randomization status were controlled for. Tobacco exposure is associated with many other health conditions and should be avoided for other reasons, regardless of our findings.

Our study findings contribute to the limited epidemiologic literature on modifiable risk factors and prospective risk of FECD. In this sample of postmenopausal women, personal smoking and SHS exposure were not significantly associated with an increased risk of FECD. However, our findings indicated there may be an association between tobacco exposure and FECD but limitations in the variation of tobacco exposure in WHI, survival bias, and sick quitter effects may have contributed to wide CIs. Despite these challenges, our study is one of the most robust analyses to date regarding tobacco exposure and risk of FECD among older women. Modifiable exposures warrant further exploration in relation to FECD risk and severity.
